REV86

Greatest Events of the Eighties: EDSA@40
This film is dedicated to the Filipino people who stood against tyranny and oppression of the Marcos dictatorship.

What is REV86?

REV86 is an interactive website that educates students about the EDSA People Power Revolution through a multimedia approach. It aims to provide accessible, easy to understand information to future researchers as well as educators who wish to teach EDSA. The website features the narrative film in the main page as the opener before every feature, which will be followed by additional content such as interactive timelines leading up, during, and the immediate aftermath of EDSA, key historical figures' profiles that were involved with the era, archival photos, and fast facts to engage students in understanding the historical event.

The platform will serve as a digital learning tool for students not just in La Salle Green Hills, but also other schools in and out of the country, as part of an effort to promote critical thinking against historical distortion and the importance of preserving factual history in the digital age.

To put it simply, REV86 was created to help students engage with the history of the People Power Revolution through film and interactive digital storytelling.

Our Goals

  • To educate students about the EDSA People Power Revolution in an engaging, simple yet composite interactive format.
  • To provide detailed and accessible information to future researchers without the need to conduct deep studies or investigations.
  • To promote historical authenticity, combat misinformation and distortion and encourage higher community engagement and interaction regarding the EDSA Revolution.
  • To create a sustainable learning platform that can be continuously used, updated, and expanded in the long term as part of an effort to preserve history.
2,000,000

VOICES UNITED

Interactive Timeline

1965
1965, Philippines. Ferdinand Marcos is elected president. The country looks toward a new era of leadership. Will it bring progress or repression?

Key Historical Figures

Corazon "Cory" Aquino

  • Widow of Benigno Aquino Jr.
  • Became the symbol of the opposition after Ninoy's assassination in 1983.
  • Though she had little political experience, public pressure encouraged her to run against Ferdinand Marcos in the 1986 snap presidential election.
  • Became the first female President of the Philippines after the revolution.

Benigno "Ninoy" Aquino Jr.

  • Served as senator and governor of Tarlac; widely viewed as a potential presidential rival to Marcos.
  • Imprisoned during Martial Law and later exiled to the U.S.
  • Assassinated at the Manila International Airport on August 21, 1983 upon his return.
  • His death sparked nationwide protests that eventually led to the decisive 1986 Revolution.

Ferdinand Marcos

  • Served as president from 1965 until 1986.
  • Declared martial law in 1972, citing communist threats and civil unrest.
  • Thousands of political opponents were arrested and press freedoms restricted during Martial Law.
  • Called a snap presidential election in February 1986 amid growing opposition.
  • Allegations of electoral fraud intensified public protests, culminating in the EDSA Revolution.
  • Fled the Philippines on February 25, 1986 and went into exile in Hawaii.

Imelda Marcos

  • Not only the First Lady but also Governor of Metro Manila and Minister of Human Settlements.
  • Played a major role in the political structure of the Marcos administration.
  • Known internationally for her lavish lifestyle and cultural projects.
  • Remained with her husband in Malacañang Palace during the final days before evacuation to Hawaii.

Jovito Salonga

  • Respected lawyer, former senator, and survivor of political persecution during martial law.
  • Chaired UNIDO (United Nationalist Democratic Organization), the coalition uniting opposition groups against Marcos.
  • Supported Corazon Aquino's presidential campaign and helped coordinate the opposition's political strategy.

Jose W. Diokno

  • Former senator and one of the most prominent human rights advocates in the Philippines.
  • Arrested and detained without charges after martial law was declared in 1972.
  • Founded the Free Legal Assistance Group (FLAG), which defended victims of political persecution.
  • His work helped expose abuses during the dictatorship.

Joker Arroyo

  • Lawyer and human rights advocate.
  • Legal counsel for opposition figures and later Executive Secretary under Cory Aquino.
  • Received the Philippine Bar Association's Most Distinguished Award for Justice as a "man beholden to no one except to his country."
  • Senate Resolution No. 100 in the 8th Congress cited his invaluable service to the Filipino people.

Salvador "Doy" Laurel

  • Opposition leader and running mate of Cory Aquino in the 1986 election.
  • Later became Vice President of the Philippines.

Juan Ponce Enrile

  • Secretary of National Defense under Ferdinand Marcos.
  • On February 22, 1986, publicly withdrew support from Marcos after a planned coup by reformist officers was discovered.
  • Barricaded himself in Camp Aguinaldo and called for military support, triggering the start of the EDSA People Power uprising.

Fidel V. Ramos

  • Vice Chief of Staff of the Armed Forces and Chief of the Philippine Constabulary.
  • Joined Enrile in breaking away from Marcos and secured Camp Crame, encouraging military units to defect.
  • His support split the Armed Forces of the Philippines, weakening Marcos' control over the military.

Jaime Cardinal Sin

  • Archbishop of Manila and one of the most influential religious leaders in the country.
  • Through Radio Veritas, appealed to Filipinos to go to EDSA and protect Enrile and Ramos from loyalist troops.
  • His call mobilized thousands of civilians and transformed the rebellion into a massive peaceful protest.

Gregorio "Gringo" Honasan

  • Colonel and leader of the Reform the Armed Forces Movement (RAM).
  • Organized a coup against Marcos due to dissatisfaction with corruption and favoritism in the military.
  • When the coup plot was discovered, it forced Enrile and Ramos to openly rebel, setting the stage for the EDSA uprising.
  • Later staged more rebellions, most notably against Cory on August 28, 1987.

Col. Antonio Sotelo

  • Commander of the Philippine Air Force 15th Strike Wing based at Villamor Air Base.
  • On February 24, 1986, defected to Camp Crame with armed helicopters, refusing orders to attack rebel camps.

Brig. Gen. Artemio Tadiar

  • Commander of a Philippine Marine Brigade loyal to Marcos.
  • His armored units moved toward EDSA during the crisis to confront protesters.
  • Despite the tense situation, his forces did not launch a violent assault on civilian crowds, helping prevent large-scale bloodshed.

Maj. Gen. Fabian Ver

  • Chief of Staff of the Armed Forces of the Philippines and one of Marcos' closest military allies.
  • Remained loyal to Marcos during the revolution and oversaw loyalist military operations.
  • Divisions within the military prevented him from effectively suppressing the uprising.

Agapito "Butz" Aquino

  • Brother of Benigno Aquino Jr. and an opposition activist.
  • Helped organize some of the first civilian gatherings along EDSA, encouraging people to support the defecting military officers and protest the Marcos regime.

Lorenzo Tañada

  • Veteran nationalist leader, former senator, and long-time critic of Marcos.
  • Played a major role in organizing anti-dictatorship protests and helped lead the Justice for Aquino, Justice for All (JAJA) movement.

Hilario Davide Jr.

  • Lawyer and constitutional expert; became a member of the 1986 Constitutional Commission under the Aquino administration.
  • Helped draft the 1987 Philippine Constitution, which restored democratic institutions and safeguards after the Marcos dictatorship.

Paul Laxalt

  • United States senator from Nevada and close ally of U.S. President Ronald Reagan.
  • During the final hours of the crisis, reportedly told Marcos in a phone call to "cut and cut cleanly," advising him to step down peacefully and avoid further violence.

Archive: Myths & Facts

Myth 1

MYTH

Benigno "Ninoy" Aquino Jr. was killed by his own relatives or political allies as part of a secret plot.

FACT

Multiple official investigations found no credible evidence of family/ally involvement. The Agrava Fact-Finding Board (1983–1984) concluded the killing was a military conspiracy involving members of the Philippine Armed Forces.

Myth 2

MYTH

EDSA was just a political fight between Marcos and Cory Aquino.

FACT

EDSA was the result of a national movement including students, workers, clergy, journalists, business groups, human-rights lawyers, opposition politicians, and ordinary people. Reducing EDSA to "Marcos vs. Cory" erases the role of millions of Filipinos.

Myth 3

MYTH

EDSA started suddenly in February 1986.

FACT

The four-day uprising happened February 22–25, 1986, but its roots go much deeper — from Martial Law in 1972, to Ninoy's 1983 assassination, to the disputed 1986 Snap Elections, and the failed coup attempt by Ramos and Enrile.

Myth 4

MYTH

The snap election alone caused EDSA.

FACT

The 1986 snap election was the immediate spark, but the deeper causes were dictatorial rule, alleged human-rights abuses, the numerous Marcos cronies, and economic downfall under the Marcos regime.

Myth 5

MYTH

The military was solidly behind Marcos until the very end.

FACT

Enrile and Ramos withdrew support; RAM officers had already been plotting; and Col. Sotelo's air force defection showed Marcos no longer had uncontested command.

Myth 6

MYTH

EDSA was only a military coup with civilians used as shields.

FACT

The outcome changed because huge civilian crowds willingly protected the defectors. Without the public, Enrile and Ramos could have been crushed easily in less than 24 hours.

Myth 7

MYTH

The Revolution happened only on one stretch of highway in Manila.

FACT

The symbolic center was EDSA between Camp Crame and Camp Aguinaldo, but support was broader — Aquino and Laurel were in Cebu City early on, while cities like Davao demonstrated opposition support.

Myth 8

MYTH

The United States orchestrated EDSA.

FACT

EDSA was started by Filipinos, not any foreign power. The U.S. closely monitored events and pressure on Marcos mattered in the final hours, but the movement was driven by the Filipino people.

Myth 9

MYTH

EDSA instantly fixed the Philippines.

FACT

EDSA ended Marcos' dictatorship and restored democratic institutions, but did not magically solve poverty, inequality, or corruption. Inflation continued and power outages became common during Cory's presidency.

Myth 10

MYTH

Because problems remained after 1986, EDSA accomplished nothing.

FACT

EDSA removed Marcos, ended dictatorial rule, restored democratic institutions, and produced the 1987 Constitution. It is fair to criticize post-EDSA governments; it is false to claim nothing changed.

Myth 11

MYTH

Martial Law was separate from EDSA and should not be linked to it.

FACT

EDSA cannot be understood without Martial Law. The uprising was, in large part, a direct response to the oppressive system Marcos built after 1972.

Fast Facts

Radio Veritas Was CrucialThe Catholic radio station broadcast instructions and updates guiding civilians. When its transmitter was attacked, it secretly continued broadcasting from a backup in Bulacan.
The Coup Was the Real Turning PointThe revolution truly began after Enrile and Ramos defected from Marcos, seeking protection from the public at Camp Aguinaldo and Camp Crame after their failed coup d'état attempt.
Marcos Believed It Would FailEarly intelligence reports underestimated the crowd size, and Marcos reportedly thought the demonstrations were manageable protests.
An Assault That Never HappenedMarcos ordered General Ver to launch an attack on Camp Crame, but several officers hesitated or delayed the order, helping prevent a massacre.
No Single OrganizerUnlike most uprisings, EDSA was not centrally organized. The massive crowd formed spontaneously over 4 days after Cardinal Sin's radio appeal.
Yellow: Symbol of OppositionYellow became the symbol of the Aquinos following Ninoy's 1983 assassination, inspired by the song "Tie a Yellow Ribbon Round the Ole Oak Tree." Most famously worn by Cory Aquino during EDSA.
"Bayan Ko" — The Revolution's AnthemProtesters frequently sang "Bayan Ko," deemed seditious under Martial Law. It was sung at Aquino's 1983 funeral and subsequently at EDSA by many protesters.
Only Four DaysFrom February 22 to 25, 1986, Marcos' 20-year rule ended in just 96 hours of mass civilian resistance.
Foreign Governments Were WatchingThe United States, especially the Reagan administration, played a role in pressuring Marcos to step down and leave the country.
Laxalt's Decisive MessageU.S. Senator Paul Laxalt reportedly told Marcos by phone: "I think you should cut and cut cleanly," meaning he should step down and leave.
The U.S. Spearheaded Marcos' EvacuationU.S. helicopters evacuated Marcos and his family from Malacañang to Clark Air Base, then to Hawaii, to facilitate a peaceful transition of power to Cory.
Dramatic Helicopter DefectionSeveral PAF helicopters landed at Camp Crame — first thought to be an assault — led by Colonel Antonio Sotelo of the 15th Strike Wing, who convinced his pilots to join the rebellion.
Two Inaugurations in One DayOn February 25, Marcos held an inauguration at Malacañang Palace, while Corazon Aquino was sworn in separately at Club Filipino in San Juan.
Inspired Movements WorldwideThe EDSA People Power Revolution became a global model for peaceful democratic revolutions, influencing movements in Eastern Europe and most famously the Fall of the Berlin Wall.
An Unfinished RevolutionSome historians describe EDSA as an "unfinished revolution" — a major democratic breakthrough that restored formal democracy but did not resolve deeper structural inequalities in Philippine society.
